coredata.dc:title: Visual detection of cyclobutane pyrimidine dimer DNA damage lesions by Hg2+ and carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Singh, Seema
Singh, Manoj K.
Das, Prolay
coredata.dc:description: Cyclobutane pyrimidine dimmers (CPDs) and 6–4-[pyrimidine-2′-one] pyrimidine (6-4 PP) are major UV induced DNA damage lesions formed from solar radiation and other sources. CPD lesions are presumably mutagenic and carcinogenic that inhibit polymerases and interfere in DNA replication. An easy and cost effective way for visual detection of these lesions by using fluorescence based method is shown here. Artificial UVA and UVB lights were used for the generation of CPD and 6-4 PPs in selected DNA samples. Binding of Hg2+ ions with DNA before and after induction of CPD and 6-4 PP lesions was evaluated in the presence of highly fluorescent blue emitting carbon dots (CDs). Induction of CPD and 6-4 PPs in DNA causes distortion of DNA structure which hinders the binding of Hg2+ ions to DNA nucleobases. Quenching of fluorescence intensity of CDs by unbound Hg2+ ions was found to be proportional to the amount of CPD and 6-4 PP lesions induced by UV irradiation of DNA samples that offer a biosensing platform for the sensitive detection of CPD lesions in DNA. The fluorescent quenching was visually detectable using hand held UV light without the intervention of any equipment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cyclobutane pyrimidine dimer
DNA
Carbon dots
Mercury
Fluorescence
UV light

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV irradiation of DNA for CPD formation and analysis by T4 endonuclease V
Quantitative estimation of amine functional group on CDs
Interaction of Hg2+ with DNA and CDs
UV induced CPD generation and analysis
Detection of CPD in dsDNA by Hg2+ and CDs
Fluorescence quenching of CD related to time of UV irradiation
Conformational analysis and binding interaction of DNA with Hg2+
Interaction of different metal ions with CDs for CPD detection
Estimation of CPD formation in pUC19

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by a grant to P. D. and M. K. Singh from BRNS ( 2012/34/59/BRNS/2862 ), Department of Atomic Energy (DAE), Govt. of India, Project No.2012/34/59/BRNS. S. S. is thankful to BRNS and IIT Patna for fellowship. The authors are thankful to IIT Patna, BARC (Mumbai) and SAIF-Punjab university for infrastructure and other facilities. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Proof of CPD generation through UV irradiation and fragmentation at CPD by T4 endonuclease A. 20% PAGE showing fragmentation of oligonucleotide B. 1.5% agarose gel showing fragmentation of pUC19 plasmid. C. Thermal melting and D. First derivative of thermal melting showing change in thermal melting temperature of DNA with increase in UVB irradiation time.
A. UV–visible absorbance B. Fluorescence spectra C. TEM D. XRD of CDs.

Change in fluorescence intensity of CDs with increasing concentration of Hg2+ A. Steady-state fluorescence Spectra B. Corresponding digital images and C. Time-resolved fluorescence decay of CD and CD-Hg2+ complex.

Effect of Hg2+-DNA (nonirradiated) on fluorescence intensity of CDs A. Fluorescence spectra B. Corresponding digital images, C. Comparative change in fluorescence intensity of CDs with Hg2+ and Hg2+-DNA (irradiated & nonirradiated) and D. Corresponding digital images.
Effect of increasing irradiation of DNA (in DNA-Hg2+) on fluorescence intensity of CDs A. Fluorescence spectra and B. Corresponding digital images of sample.
Binding interaction of Hg2+ ions with nucleobases of normal and UV irradiated DNA.

Circular dichroism spectra showing conformational changes for Hg2+ binding in A. dsDNA (nonirradiated) B. dsDNA (UVA) and C. dsDNA (UVB) and Isothermal titration calorimetry graph for Hg2+ binding in D. dsDNA (nonirradiated) E. dsDNA (UVA) and F. dsDNA (UVB).

Effect of different metal ions on fluorescence intensity of CDs A. Fluorescence spectra B. Corresponding digital images; C. Comparative Effect of DNA-Hg2+ and DNA-Cu2+ (with and without CPD) on fluorescence intensity of CDs and D. Corresponding digital images.
A. Decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs with Hg2+ and Hg2+-pUC19 (irradiated & nonirradiated) and B. Corresponding digital image.
UV induced CPD detection in DNA using Hg2+ and CDs.
Oligomeric DNA sequences used for template formation.

row.entry: 5′- AGCT GCG CGA AAA CAC GTG CAC ATCTTTTCA GAT ACGCG -3′
3′- CGC GCT TTT GTG CAC GTG TAG AAA AGT CTA TGC GC-5′

head.ce:title: Visual detection of cyclobutane pyrimidine dimer DNA damage lesions by Hg2+ and carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, Patna, 801106, Bihar, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Atomic and Molecular Physics Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Mumbai, 400085, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cyclobutane pyrimidine dimmers (CPDs) and 6–4-[pyrimidine-2′-one] pyrimidine (6-4 PP) are major UV induced DNA damage lesions formed from solar radiation and other sources. CPD lesions are presumably mutagenic and carcinogenic that inhibit polymerases and interfere in DNA replication. An easy and cost effective way for visual detection of these lesions by using fluorescence based method is shown here. Artificial UVA and UVB lights were used for the generation of CPD and 6-4 PPs in selected DNA samples. Binding of Hg2+ ions with DNA before and after induction of CPD and 6-4 PP lesions was evaluated in the presence of highly fluorescent blue emitting carbon dots (CDs). Induction of CPD and 6-4 PPs in DNA causes distortion of DNA structure which hinders the binding of Hg2+ ions to DNA nucleobases. Quenching of fluorescence intensity of CDs by unbound Hg2+ ions was found to be proportional to the amount of CPD and 6-4 PP lesions induced by UV irradiation of DNA samples that offer a biosensing platform for the sensitive detection of CPD lesions in DNA. The fluorescent quenching was visually detectable using hand held UV light without the intervention of any equipment.

ce:section.ce:para: Cyclobutane Pyrimidine Dimer (CPD) and 6–4-[pyrimidine-2′-one] pyrimidine photoproducts (6-4 PPs) in DNA are UV light induced DNA damage lesions formed by covalent linking of adjacent pyrimidine bases [1–3]. Exposure to UV-A & B light reaches the surface of earth from Sun and could effectively generate CPDs and 6-4 PPs in our DNA [4–6]. The comparative rate for CPD production is ∼90% while it is ∼10% for 6-4 PP [7,8]. Pyrimidine dimers are the major cause of mutation of cytosine (C) to thymine (T), cell killing, immunosuppression and carcinogenesis [9–11]. Enhanced exposure to UV radiation results in the profound rise in CPD production with slow repair that are related to skin related diseases including induction of melanoma and skin cancer in humans [12–14]. Reportedly, p53 mutations (C to T conversion) in TP53 gene of Xeroderma Pigmentosum patients have decreased repair efficiency of these lesions underlining the important role of CPD in melanoma [15,16]. Thus, detecting these mutagenic pyrimidine dimers in DNA is essential to diagnose premature aging, skin cancer and maintenance of genome integrity [17–19]. Consequently, development of tools to identify these DNA lesions is of broad scientific interest. Herein, we report an easy, economic and sensitive method for visual detection of the presence of pyrimidine dimer lesions in DNA by using mercury (Hg2+) and carbon dots (CDs) as an effective solution phase biosensing platform.

Numerous methods have been adopted for detection of CPD such as flow cytometry, radioimmunoassay (RIA), enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), alkaline gel electrophoresis by T4 endonuclease V, mass spectrometry, Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) and near-infrared spectroscopy [20–24]. However, multistep tedious process, time consumption and costly devices may limit their applications and practical biomedical utility. This essentially necessitates the development of a simple yet reproducible strategy for the detection of these DNA damage lesions. Recently, graphene oxide has been utilised for detection of UV induced mutagenic thymine dimers [25]. The detection strategy requires conjugation of DNA with fluorophores that are subsequently quenched to give a signal. In the current study, we have used CDs as a fluorescent probe to track the differential binding of Hg2+ ions with normal and CPD lesion containing DNA.

The interaction of DNA nucleobases and metal ions has emerged as an area of focus for quite some time [26–28]. The transition metal ions preferably bind with the donor atoms of heterocyclic nucleobases unlike the counter ions (Mg2+ and Na+) of DNA that interact with oxygen atoms of phosphate backbone of DNA [29]. Among the transition metal ions, binding of Hg2+ to DNA's nucleobases particularly with thymine (T) and then cytosine (C) is remarkable [30]. Specific interaction of Hg2+ ions with pyrimidine nucleobases have shown promising applications in DNA nanotechnology such as single nucleotide polymorphism detection, ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ in drinking water and DNA assembly formation [31–33]. The source of Hg2+ being easily available low cost mercury salts, it instigates special interest to explore this metal ion for broad biomedical applications.

Carbon derived nanoparticles like CDs, carbon nanotubes, graphene etc. have shown diverse physical, chemical and optical properties. In particular, CDs are considered as an effective alternative to semiconductor quantum dots and other metal based nanoparticles [34]. Low cost convenient route of synthesis, biocompatibility and high fluorescent properties of CDs pave the way for its use in biosensing, drug delivery, catalysis, electronic applications etc [35–37]. The intense fluorescence of CDs are quenched by Hg2+ -a well-known electron acceptor which can interact with CDs through electrostatic interaction that prompt the ultrasensitive detection of the latter [38]. In the present study, the interaction of Hg2+ ions with DNA and CDs were put together to develop a biosensing method for the detection of UV induced damage in DNA, in particular CPD and 6-4 PP lesions. The CPD and 6-4 PP lesions in T rich oligonucleotide and pUC19 were created on irradiation by using UVA and UVB lamps. The presence of CPD in oligonucleotide and pUC19 plasmid DNA introduces conformational distortion of the DNA that results in poor interaction of Hg2+ with these UV irradiated dsDNA. The exclusion of Hg2+ from DNA and their subsequent presence in solution quantitatively quench the fluorescence of CDs (Scheme 1 ) that is visually detectable following irradiation with a handheld UV lamp only.

Citric acid monohydrate, glycine, mercury (II) acetate Hg(OAc)2 were purchased from Sigma. pUC19 plasmid, HindIII restriction endonuclease and T4 PDG (T4 endonuclease V) were purchased from New England Biolabs, USA. Nanopure water from Millipore was used in all experiments. The oligomeric DNA sequences were obtained from Sigma custom oligo service (Table 1 ). For all measurements, DNA concentration is given in terms of concentration of nucleobases.

Irradiation of DNA samples with UV lamps was done in photoreactor (Luzchem, LZC-4X, Canada). Formation of CPD in oligomer DNA on UV irradiation was observed by 20% polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) in Tris Taurine EDTA (TTE) buffer. Similarly, for pUC19 the lesion was analyzed by 1.5% agarose gel electrophoresis in tris acetate EDTA (TAE) buffer at 100 V and images were captured using a CCD camera fitted with a gel documentation system (UVP, UK). Absorption spectra of samples in UV–visible range were measured on an UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Steady-state fluorescence spectra were obtained on Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba, Japan). For transmission electron microscopy (TEM), a dilute solution of CDs (0.5 mg/mL) were dispersed on a carbon grid and captured through Hitachi (H-7500) electron microscope at 100 kV accelerating voltage. The lattice spacing and crystalline phase of CDs were analyzed through X-ray diffractometer (XRD). Hydrodynamic radii and zeta potential of samples were measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) on a Delsa™ Nano particle analyzer (Beckman Coulter). Surface functionality of CDs were measured by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Shimadzu, Japan). CDs were analyzed with X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) (Oxford Instrument Germany) equipped with monochromator Aluminum Source (Al Kα radiation hv = 1486.7ev) at operating voltage of 15 kV and 20 mA. Conformational change of DNA on interaction with Hg2+ was measured by circular dichroism spectra on a J-1500 spectropolarimeter (Jasco, UK) by scanning the sample thrice at the scan rate of 50 nm/min in a quartz cell with 1 mm path length. The stability of dsDNA was measured by the thermal melting temperature before and after UV irradiation using a Peltier controlled UV–vis spectrophotometer (Bioquest, Cecil, UK). Digital images were captured with a Canon D600 camera. All experiments were performed in triplicate for consistency. To avoid the mercury contamination in environment, all mercury containing samples were disposed in biohazards waste box and supplied to waste treatment plants.

To determine the thermodynamic parameters of binding event of Hg2+ with DNA, isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) studies were done using MicroCal ITC200 (GE, USA). For analysis, 2 μL aliquots of Hg (OAc)2 solution (40 μL, 200 μM) was titrated against DNA solution (280 μL, 20 μM) in MOPS buffer with constant stirring speed of 700 rpm at 25 °C and pH 7 for 20 injections. The titration was performed by keeping the initial molar ratio of DNA and Hg2+ = 1:10 where DNA act as a ligand for Hg2+ as an acceptor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV irradiation of DNA for CPD formation and analysis by T4 endonuclease V
ce:section.ce:para: Induction of CPD in DNA by UV irradiation was carried out in a photoreactor with multilamp sockets. The DNA sample was sealed in quartz cuvette and was placed at the centre of the photoreactor. For irradiation, 8 W UV lamps (UVA-365 nm and UVB-302 nm) were used and the irradiated sample was collected for different interval of time.

The CPD formation in DNA samples were analyzed by using T4 PDG (T4 endonuclease V) enzyme. The non-irradiated control and irradiated DNA (0.5 μg) samples were incubated with two units of T4 enzyme in 1X T4 PDG buffer for 30 min. After enzyme incubation, all the samples were purified by ethanol wash and analyzed on 20% denaturing PAGE for oligonucleotide and 1.5% agarose gel for pUC19 plasmid.

N-doped CDs were prepared by hydrothermal heating of Citric acid and Glycine as a carbon source and nitrogen source respectively, following the method reported by Dong et al. with some modifications [39]. In a typical CD synthesis, mixture of citric acid (8 mmol) and glycine (8 mmol) were solubilized in 10 mL water and heated at 150 °C for 8 h in a hydrothermal reactor. The autoclave was allowed to cool naturally and the solution was diluted and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm to separate the large agglomerated particles along with unreacted organic moieties from CDs. The brown aqueous solution containing CDs was collected and further filtered through 0.2 μm filter for refinement and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative estimation of amine functional group on CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Quantification of amino group on surface of CDs was carried out through Ninhydrin and standard solution of glycine. For analysis, a stock of 8% w/v of Ninhydrin in acetone and glycine solution 150 μg/mL in 0.2 M acetate buffer was prepared. Different volume of standard glycine (0.1 mL–1 mL) and CDs (1 mL, 150 μg/mL) was added to distilled water to make total volume of 4 mL followed by addition of 1 mL Ninhydrin to it. All solutions were covered and heated in boiling water bath for 15 min and cooled down to room temperature. 1 mL of ethanol (50% v/v) was added to this solution and absorbance of all solutions was measured at 570 nm to determine the unknown concentration of amine group on CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of Hg2+ with DNA and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction of Hg2+ ions with DNA (100 nM, irradiated and non-irradiated) was carried out by adding different amounts of Hg2+ to DNA sample and ratio of Hg2+:DNA was maintained in the range of 0.2:1 to 1:1. Samples were incubated for 15 min to ensure the binding of Hg2+ with the DNA bases.

Similarly, interaction of Hg2+ ions with CDs (100 nM, molar ratio of amine functional group) was analyzed by increasing the ratio of Hg2+:CD, from 0.2:1 to 1:1. To track the free Hg2+ ions in solution of Hg2+-DNA (with and without CPD), CDs the tracking agent was added to it and the final concentration of CDs and DNA were maintained as 100 nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV induced CPD generation and analysis
ce:section.ce:para: UVA and UVB are the major radiations from sun that play a key role in skin cancer. As such, the genotoxic and cytotoxic potential of UV radiations are well known. In the present study, bipyrimidines in DNA samples (oligonucleotide and pUC19 plasmid DNA) were generated by irradiating the sample with UV lamps (A&B). Formation of CPD was checked by treating the DNA samples with T4 PDG that cleaves these DNA lesions and cleavage was confirmed through gel electrophoresis (Fig. 1 ) [40]. These CPD containing samples were used in further downstream studies.

The UV irradiated DNA showed decreased stability as evident from thermal melting (Tm) temperature (Fig. 1C&D). The stability decreases proportionally with increase in irradiation time and lesions in DNA [25]. To optimise the time of UV irradiation required for complete CPD formation in a given DNA sample, UVB irradiation was done from 15 min to 60 min and the Tm of all samples were measured. The Tm of normal oligomer DNA is 65.5 °C that records a continuous decrease as irradiation time was increased indicative of decreased thermal stability of the DNA with CPD formation. However, change in Tm was observed up to 1 h of irradiation that does not change significantly beyond that. Hence, further downstream studies were limited to 1 h of irradiation.

Numerous methods have been adopted for synthesis of CDs such as laser ablation, irradiation through microwave and electrochemical oxidation. However, synthesis of CDs through hydrothermal heating of precursors is an easy, green and economic method which provides CDs with high quantum yield. Herein, N-doped CDs were synthesized using citric acid as a carbon source and glycine as a source of nitrogen. The approximate amine functional group on the surface of CDs were estimated by using Ninhydrin which forms purple colour complex when added to CDs indicating its amine functionality [41]. The concentration of amine group was found to be ∼2.6 mmol/g of CDs [42,43]. Optical characterization of CDs was carried out through UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. It shows maximum absorption at 330 nm which can be assigned to n- π* transition due to CO functional group (Fig. 2 A). The fluorescence spectra of CDs show maximum fluorescence intensity at 440 nm at excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Moreover, the emission of CDs was found to be excitation wavelength dependent and ranged from 420 to 460 nm when excitation was changed from 300 to 400 nm (Fig. 2 B). The quantum yield of CDs has been measured using the standard sample of quinine sulphate (QY = 0.55) in 0.5 M H2SO4 and was found to be 42%. The strong fluorescence intensity of CDs may be attributed to trapping of excited state energy and electron-hole recombination in N-state due to doping.

Shape and size of CDs were measured through TEM which reveals size distribution in the range of 2–6 nm in diameter with spherical shape (Fig. 2C). XRD pattern of CDs yielded one strong peak at 2θ = 28.24° and another shoulder peak at 2θ = 41.98° which indicate interlayer distance of 0.32 nm and 0.21 nm respectively (Fig. 2D). This lattice spacing of 0.32 nm and 0.21 nm suggest the (002) and (101) diffraction pattern similar to graphitic carbon of CDs [44].

The surface functionality of CDs was analyzed and FTIR spectrum is shown in Fig. S1. From spectrum analysis, characteristic peaks were observed at 3562 cm−1, 3293 cm−1, 2923 cm−1, 1189 cm−1 and 1075 cm−1 corresponding to -OH, -NH, -CH, -C-N and -C-O stretching vibrations respectively. Two bands at 1722 and 1607 cm−1 can be assigned to –CO asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations respectively and confirms the formation of amide bond [45]. To further verify the presence of these functional groups on CDs, XPS was carried out. The full scan of XPS spectrum exhibited characteristic peaks at 284.6, 399.8 and 531.45 eV attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s respectively (Fig. S2). The XPS spectrum was deconvoluted for each element to analyze the characteristic peak for functional groups. C1s spectrum shows presence of three peaks at 284.7, 286.3 and 288.66 eV which are attributed to C-C/CC, C-N/C-O and CN/CO respectively [46,47]. The N1s spectrum of CDs showed peak at 399.83 eV for –N-H bond. O1s spectrum revealed peaks at 531.42 and 535.3 eV which can be assigned to CO and C-O respectively. The results obtained from FTIR and XPS spectra confirm the presence of hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of CDs which enhance its water dispersibility.

Reportedly, N-doped CDs have been utilized for detection of Hg2+ where quenching of CDs was attributed to affinity of Hg2+ for nitrogen and subsequent complex formation with CDs through O and N [48]. The quenching of CDs in presence of Hg2+ due to electron transfer between Hg2+ and CDs have been used for the detection of the former by a fluorescent on-off based method [49]. By considering the interaction of metal ions with functional group of CDs, we utilized the interaction of Hg2+ with CD's amine group that could modulate the fluorescence of CDs. CD solution (100 nM; conc. of CD's amine groups) was titrated with Hg2+ by increasing the molar ratio of Hg2+:CD from 0.2:1 to 1:1. A significant decrease in fluorescence intensity of CD was observed that is proportional to the increasing concentration of Hg2+ ions (Fig. 3 A) and at 1:1 Hg2+: CD ratio, quenching of CD's fluorescence was ∼90%. The fluorescence quenching of CDs through Hg2+ can be attributed to the non-radiative recombination of exciton due to electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged CDs and Hg2+ ions as well as stable complex formation of Hg2+ with CDs through O and N atoms on its surface [50,51]. Further, digital images of the samples indicate a detectable visual change in the fluorescent intensity of CDs when illuminated with a UV lamp. Full quenching of CD fluorescence was observed at 0.8:1 ratio of Hg2+: CD and beyond from the digital images.

The quenching mechanism of CDs by Hg2+ ions was analyzed by measuring the fluorescence lifetime, hydrodynamic size and zeta potential of CDs after Hg2+ addition to it. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs was found to get reduced from 7.1 ns to 5.7 ns on interaction with Hg2+ (Fig. 3C). This decrease in lifetime of CDs may be attributed to nonradiative electron transfer to Hg2+ ions due to dynamic quenching of CDs [52]. From DLS data it was observed that average hydrodynamic size of CDs was increased from 6 nm to 14 nm on addition of Hg2+ ions (Fig. S3). In addition to that, the zeta potential of CDs get increased from −4.66 to −0.21 (Fig. S4) after interaction with Hg2+. This point towards complex formation of Hg2+ with CDs due to availability of vacant d-orbitals on Hg2+ for chelation with amine functional groups on the surface of CDs [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CPD in dsDNA by Hg2+ and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Reportedly, metal ions bind to DNA through electron donation from nucleobases [30]. In some cases, complex formation via π-π interaction has been also observed [54]. Metal-DNA interactions have been taken advantage of for hosts of detection strategies [55]. Hg2+ had striking contribution to various applications that are based on metal-nucleobases interactions [56]. Hg2+ shows strong affinity for nucleobases particularly with thymine (T) [57]. Taking advantage of this affinity, the differential interaction of Hg2+ with normal DNA and UV irradiated DNA having CPD lesions is amplified to provide a visually detectable output in this study. The binding of Hg2+ ions with DNA was analyzed after incubating mercury acetate solution (from 20 nM to 100 nM) with DNA for 15 min, with increasing ratio of Hg2+: DNA from 0.2:1 to 1:1, followed by addition of CDs. CD's fluorescence was found to be unaffected as long as the ratio of Hg2+:DNA (normal, unirradiated): CD was kept 0.8:1:1. However, ∼20% decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs was observed when ratio of Hg2+:DNA:CD was increased to 1:1:1 (Fig. 4 A &B). This indicate that initial Hg2+ ions concentration need to be maintained in such a manner to ensure complete encapsulation in the DNA in this detection strategy. This confirms the absence of excess free Hg2+ ions in the solution that could interact with the amine groups of CD and results in fluorescence quenching in the control sample.

The presence of CPD in DNA resulting from UV irradiation impose poor binding of Hg2+ ions to nucleobases due to conformational distortion of DNA [58]. Evidently, few Hg2+ ions that otherwise bind to DNA in UV irradiated DNA samples are ejected from the DNA and released in solution. These Hg2+ ions in solution in turn quench the fluorescence of CD quantitatively. Higher quenching of fluorescence of CDs by Hg2+ for UVB irradiated DNA samples as compared to UVA was observed which reflect more CPD formation in former (Fig. 4C and D). The visual detection of CPD was investigated by varying the concentration of DNA (UVB irradiated) from 50 nM to 0.25 nM and it was observed upto 1 nM of DNA in DNA-Hg2+, the change in fluorescence intensity of CDs is visually detectable (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of CD related to time of UV irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to thermal melting studies of DNA, the effect of irradiation time on CPD formation was also analyzed by measuring the change in fluorescence intensity of CDs. DNA that are UV irradiated (UV B) for selective time interval (0–60 min) were incubated with predetermined amount of Hg2+. These solutions were further added to CDs solution to determine changes in fluorescence intensity.

With increase in irradiation time, the concentration of CPD lesions in DNA increases that was revealed by steady decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs (Fig. 5 A). Thus, change in fluorescence intensity of CDs is proportional to the concentration of CPD induced in DNA due to irradiation. However, a significant quenching of CDs was observed in DNA-Hg2+ sample having DNA irradiated for 60 min (Fig. 5B). The optimum irradiation time for CPD formation was found to be 60 min that correlate well with thermal melting data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conformational analysis and binding interaction of DNA with Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of DNA-metal ion complex formation through various interactions has been researched intensely [59]. Hg2+ ions have shown high affinity for nucleobases of DNA by forming a base- Hg2+ -base complex through dative bond formation (Fig. 6 ). This complex formation can be attributed to the soft-soft interaction and coordinate bond formation between Hg2+ ions and nucleobases. However, on UV irradiation cross-linking of adjacent pyrimidine bases leads to formation of CPD or 6-4 PP lesions in DNA. The two cross linked bases in these lesions lose the planar π electrons, aromaticity that induces distortion in DNA structure (Fig. 6) [58,60]. The conformational distortion subsequently lead to exclusion of Hg2+ in those lesions. Formation of DNA-Hg2+ complex at damaged site is thus prevented resulting in the quenching of CDs fluorescence due to Hg2+ ions that comes out in solution from the DNA or already in the solution due to inability to bind to DNA.

Reportedly, interaction of Hg2+ with DNA results in its conformational change from random coil to hairpin like structure [61]. The pyrimidine bases of DNA binds to Hg2+ ions and form a loop through base-Hg2+-base interactions. In our study, the conformational change in DNA templates (nonirradiated i.e. without CPD and UV A/B irradiated i.e. with CPD or 6-4 PP) on Hg2+ binding was analyzed by circular dichroism of DNA-Hg2+ complex. DNA without CPD lesions or without any irradiation showed drastic conformational change with increasing concentration of Hg2+. Hg2+ ions that bind to DNA introduce conformational change in the DNA from B-form to Z-form. The negative peak at 249 nm and positive peak at 280 nm of B-form DNA was replaced by negative peaks at 214 nm, 232 nm and 272 nm (Fig. 7 A) that are characteristic to Z-form of DNA. However, Hg2+ was unable to introduce any drastic conformational change of UVA and UVB irradiated DNA samples (Fig. 7 B&C) [62]. These findings indicate that interaction of Hg2+ ions with UV irradiated dsDNA samples get significantly reduced upon introduction of electron deficient CPD lesions.

The binding affinity of Hg2+ to dsDNA with and without CPD lesion was quantitatively evaluated by ITC. Hg (OAc)2 solution was titrated with dsDNA samples and data was fitted for single site of binding to obtain the thermodynamic parameters. Exothermic binding of Hg2+ ions with all dsDNA samples occur as evident from negative peaks in the isotherms. Normal DNA devoid of any CPD lesions shows sigmoidal pattern of decreasing enthalpy with time on binding with Hg2+ ions. This indicate specific interaction of Hg2+ with nucleobases of DNA leading to saturation of binding sites in DNA with increasing concentration of Hg2+ ions (Fig. 7 D) [63]. However, absence of saturation point in titration plots of Hg2+ with UVA/B irradiated DNA signify nonspecific binding of the ions with the DNA samples (Fig. 7 E&F). The CPD containing DNA with electron deficient nucleobases inefficiently bind with Hg2+ ions. The binding affinity constant of Hg2+ ions with UVA and UVB irradiated DNA were reduced as compared to normal DNA (Tables 1 and SI). It is thus important to note that data from thermal melting, fluorescence, circular dichroism coupled with thermodynamic parameters indicate reduced affinity of Hg2+ ions towards DNA that contain CPD lesions, which forms the basis for the detection of the later.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of different metal ions with CDs for CPD detection
ce:section.ce:para: The obvious question regarding the possibility of using any other metal ions and selectivity of CDs for Hg2+ ions in this detection strategy was also addressed. Some common metal ions Fe2+, Zn2+, Ag+ and Cu2+ were screened to assess their effect on fluorescence intensity of CDs after their binding with UV irradiated DNA. The metal ions were incubated with CDs in 0.8:1 = metal: CDs ratio and fluorescence intensity of CDs were measured in the absence and presence of the ions. From fluorescence intensity analysis, it was observed that Fe2+, Zn2+ and Ag+ shows no significant changes in fluorescence intensity of CDs (Fig. 8 A&B). Among these ions, Cu2+ was found to bind with CDs and causes ∼50% fluorescence quenching. However, at this concentration ratio, Hg2+ showed ∼80% quenching indicating their stronger binding affinity towards CDs than Cu2+ [64]. The selectivity of CDs for Hg2+ are attributed to stronger affinity of Hg2+ with CDs' electron rich surface and non-radiative electron-hole recombination as evident from fluorescence lifetime, DLS and zeta potential measurement of CD-Hg2+ complex (Fig. 3C, S3 & S4) [52].

Interaction of Cu2+ -DNA (CPD containing) with CDs was also analyzed and compared with Hg2+−DNA complex. The quenching of fluorescence of CDs in solution containing UV irradiated DNA samples having CPD lesions were ∼75% and ∼45% by Hg2+-DNA and Cu2+ -DNA respectively. Evidently, in presence of CPD, the free Hg2+ ions showed stronger affinity to CDs that is visually distinguishable (Fig. 8D). The higher affinity of Hg2+ for CDs coupled with its poor binding towards CPD lesions make Hg2+ a better candidate than Cu2+ for more sensitive CPD detection. However, contamination of DNA sample with Cu2+ ions can interfere in the proposed detection strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of CPD formation in pUC19
ce:section.ce:para: The sample scope and sensitivity of this biosensing platform for CPD detection was analyzed by observing the response in pUC19 plasmid DNA. As a proof of principle, pUC19 was irradiated with UVA and UVB for the induction of CPD lesions. Similar to oligonucleotide, effect of CPD induction on thermal stability of pUC19 and its conformational change on binding with Hg2+ was also analyzed. The results obtained from thermal melting studies and conformational changes of pUC19 (with and without CPD) on Hg2+ ions binding were in agreement to the data obtained for oligonucleotide (Figs. S6 and S7). The amount of free Hg2+ ions released in solution of CPD containing pUC19 DNA was examined by using CDs. Similar to oligonucleotide, the decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs were more in case of UVB irradiated pUC19 as compared to UVA irradiated sample (Fig. 9 ). This validates that more CPD formation in pUC19 occurs when highly energetic UVB radiations are absorbed by DNA. Most importantly, quenching of the fluorescence of CDs in the presence of CPD lesions in DNA are visually detectable with a hand held UV light.

In summary, visual detection of UVA/B induced CPD lesion in DNA was carried out using Hg2+ ions and CDs as a tracking agent. The formation of CPD in DNA was found to depend on irradiation time and 1 h of irradiation was found to induce efficient CPD induction. Hindered binding of Hg2+ ions with DNA containing CPD lesions was observed compared to normal DNA duplex. The free Hg2+ ions in CPD containing DNA samples were tracked through CDs where Hg2+ causes significant fluorescence quenching of the later. The decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs in Hg2+-DNA complex was found to be proportional to concentration of CPD lesions in DNA. While, normal DNA duplex shows significant conformational change on Hg2+ addition, the CPD containing dsDNA was less responsive to Hg2+ ions. The binding process of Hg2+ ions to DNA was found to be enthalpically driven and binding constant is higher for normal DNA than the CPD containing one. The detection of these UV induced DNA damage has been demonstrated to work well in pUC19 plasmid whereby quenching of CD fluorescence due to free Hg2+ ions in pUC19-Hg2+ complex was visually detectable. The proposed method can be applied for visual detection of CPD in DNA even with 1 nM concentration by using a handheld UV lamp. Evidently, this is an easy, fast and economic method reliable for biosensing for CPD containing DNA that could be researched further for development of other biosensing platforms.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant to P. D. and M. K. Singh from BRNS (2012/34/59/BRNS/2862), Department of Atomic Energy (DAE), Govt. of India, Project No.2012/34/59/BRNS. S. S. is thankful to BRNS and IIT Patna for fellowship. The authors are thankful to IIT Patna, BARC (Mumbai) and SAIF-Punjab university for infrastructure and other facilities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI) available: FTIR and XPS spectra of CDs, DLA and zeta potential for CDs, CD-Hg2+ complex, Digital images of change in fluorescence intensity of CDs with different concentration of (UVB irradiated) DNA-Hg2+,thermal melting of pUC19 on irradiation and circular dichroism of pUC19 on Hg2+ addition.
mmc1 mmc1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.02.029.
